message.content:
Research optimal responsive layouts for 3-column sidebars:

1. How do professional apps handle 3-column layouts on different screen sizes?
   - Apple Notes (macOS/iOS)
   - Bear Notes
   - Notion
   - Obsidian

2. What are common responsive breakpoint strategies?
   - When to collapse columns
   - Minimum widths that work on laptops vs large monitors
   - Auto-sizing vs fixed width columns

3. Specifically for a "Folders | Notes List | Editor" 3-column layout:
   - What's the minimum viable width for the folders column?
   - What's the minimum viable width for the notes list column?
   - Should columns auto-collapse on smaller screens?
   - Should there be a "mobile mode" that switches to drawer overlays?

4. Recommend specific pixel values and breakpoints for:
   - Large monitors (1920px+)
   - Standard monitors (1280-1920px)
   - Laptops (1024-1280px)
   - Tablets (768-1024px)
   - Mobile (<768px)

Focus on what provides the best UX across device sizes.

content.text: I'll research optimal responsive layouts for 3-column sidebars by examining how professional apps handle this pattern.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Obsidian sidebar layout responsive design mobile tablet"

Links: [{"title":"Sidebar - Obsidian Help","url":"https://help.obsidian.md/sidebar"},{"title":"Customize right sidebar on mobile - Help - Obsidian Forum","url":"https://forum.obsidian.md/t/customize-right-sidebar-on-mobile/82301"},{"title":"Mobile Sidebar Customization - Help - Obsidian Forum","url":"https://forum.obsidian.md/t/mobile-sidebar-customization/89991"},{"title":"Change Left Sidebar Width - Custom CSS & Theme Design - Obsidian Forum","url":"https://forum.obsidian.md/t/change-left-sidebar-width/80126"},{"title":"Phone Sidebar Usability - Feature archive - Obsidian Forum","url":"https://forum.obsidian.md/t/phone-sidebar-usability/59916"},{"title":"[Snippet] Desktop like Sidebar on Mobile - Share & showcase - Obsidian Forum","url":"https://forum.obsidian.md/t/snippet-desktop-like-sidebar-on-mobile/69710"},{"title":"Mobile Sidebar placement overrides Desktop Sidebar placement - Help - Obsidian Forum","url":"https://forum.obsidian.md/t/mobile-sidebar-placement-overrides-desktop-sidebar-placement/44591"},{"title":"Obsidian Miner, CSS for Banner Icon and Float Sidebar - Share & showcase - Obsidian Forum","url":"https://forum.obsidian.md/t/obsidian-miner-css-for-banner-icon-and-float-sidebar/57699"},{"title":"Obsidian 1.11.1 Mobile (Early access) - Obsidian","url":"https://obsidian.md/changelog/2025-12-12-mobile-v1.11.1/"},{"title":"Different pane layout on mobile and desktop? - Help - Obsidian Forum","url":"https://forum.obsidian.md/t/different-pane-layout-on-mobile-and-desktop/21580"}]

Here are the search results for "Obsidian sidebar layout responsive design mobile tablet":

## Key Findings

### Official Documentation
- Obsidian provides official documentation on sidebar functionality at help.obsidian.md.

### Mobile Sidebar Customization
- Users on Obsidian 1.6.1 can customize the right sidebar. When swiping right to left, the right sidebar opens with a drop-down containing backlinks, outgoing links, outline, and calendar options.

- On mobile iOS, users can move sidebar tabs between left and right sidebars, specifically for items like Backlinks and Calendar plugins.

### Phone vs Tablet/Desktop Differences
- On smartphones like Android Pixel 6 Pro, sidebars are organized differently compared to tablet/desktop. The left sidebar has a dedicated button, whereas the right one is only accessible via a swipe.

### CSS Customization Options
- The default minimum width of the Left Sidebar is 200px. Users can reduce the width via custom CSS, but this makes the width fixed and unadjustable.

- A CSS snippet is available to make the mobile sidebar more desktop-like by bottom-aligning the last 3 action buttons. The CSS uses `.is-mobile .side-dock-settings` with absolute positioning.

### Recent Updates (December 2024)
- Obsidian 1.11.1 Mobile (Early access) includes a refreshed design of toast messages. On phones, toasts now appear at the top of the screen instead of the bottom. There's reduced text fade at screen edges when navigation is hidden, and dialogs now have a smaller border radius on tablet.

- Recent fixes include the search bar getting cut off at the top and text overflowing from pinned sidebar into main area on tablet.

### Cross-Device Layout Sync Issues
- There's a known issue where mobile sidebar placement overrides desktop sidebar placement. When users arrange sidebar panes on desktop, open the vault on mobile, then reopen on desktop, the layouts may not remain independent.

- Users have requested different pane layouts for mobile and desktop. On desktop, layouts with 3 panes plus left and right sidebars work well for viewing multiple notes, but on mobile with limited screen real estate, this doesn't work as well.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"How to Create Multi-Column Layouts in Notion","url":"https://thomasjfrank.com/how-to-create-multi-column-layouts-in-notion/"},{"title":"How to Create Columns in Notion? (2025)","url":"https://super.so/blog/how-to-create-columns-in-notion"},{"title":"Format your page – Notion Help Center","url":"https://www.notion.com/help/columns-headings-and-dividers"},{"title":"Designing Multi-Column Layouts in Notion: A Guide to Structured Content Presentation","url":"https://www.notionry.com/faq/how-to-design-multi-column-layouts-within-notion-for-structured-content-presentation-bf971"},{"title":"Create 6-Column Layout in Notion: A Visual Guide | NotionApps","url":"https://www.notionapps.com/blog/create-6-columns-notion"},{"title":"How To Make Columns In Notion: The Ultimate Easy Guide - The Productivity Co.","url":"https://theproductivity.co/how-to-make-columns-in-notion/"},{"title":"Split Notion Page into 2 Columns | 2025 Guide | NotionApps","url":"https://www.notionapps.com/blog/split-notion-two-columns-2025"},{"title":"How To Make Columns In Notion: 2024 Guide","url":"https://www.landmarklabs.co/insights/notion-columns"},{"title":"How to Split Notion Page into Columns","url":"https://www.thebricks.com/resources/how-to-split-notion-page-into-columns"},{"title":"How to Center Text in Notion: 3 Easy Methods","url":"https://www.simple.ink/guides/how-to-center-text-in-notion-3-easy-methods"}]

Here are the search results for your query about Notion 3 column layout responsive design and breakpoints:

## Summary of Findings

### Notion Column Layouts

Notion has its own native 'columns' formatting option (which you can find in the '/' command menu) and this lets you create as many columns as you like, anywhere in your Notion pages.(no content)You can type "/column" in your Notion page, and Notion will present you with a list of four options to choose from: 2, 3, 4, or 5 columns.

### Responsive Behavior

While Notion is responsive — automatically realigning content to display on different device screen sizes, sometimes this function does not work too well. To improve mobile readability, consider simplifying column structures or using alternative layouts.(no content)Columns are available on tablet but not mobile. On your phone, you'll see the content in any right-hand column simply placed under the content in the left column.

### Responsive Design Considerations

The column method is relatively responsive and works across different screen sizes, but it may not always achieve perfect centering.(no content)The column layout offers responsive design that works well across different devices and screen sizes, keeping the text visually centered.(no content)Multiple rows improve readability and prevent overcrowding, especially on smaller screens, while a single row may look stretched on desktops.

### Customization Limitations

There is some pre-configured default padding which you can't change within columns. So, if you wanted to get two columns' content truly side-by-side, you won't have full control over the distance between them—as this is a preset feature within Notion.

### Tips for Better Responsive Layouts

Toggles can hide pieces of multi-column layouts in order to keep them more organized and easier to navigate (especially when you're using Notion's mobile apps).(no content)You can use toggles or collapsible sections inside columns to streamline detailed content.

Note: The search results did not provide specific information about numeric breakpoint values (like pixel widths) that Notion uses for responsive design. Notion handles responsiveness automatically without exposing specific breakpoint settings to users.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "Apple Notes 3 column layout responsive design macOS iOS"

Links: [{"title":"iOS and iPadOS 14: The MacStories Review - Page 12 of 23 - MacStories","url":"https://www.macstories.net/stories/ios-and-ipados-14-the-macstories-review/12/"},{"title":"How to resize the table columns in the NO… - Apple Community","url":"https://discussions.apple.com/thread/253022007"},{"title":"Apple Notes: How to Resize Table Columns or Rows","url":"https://www.alphr.com/apple-notes-resize-table-columns-rows/"},{"title":"Column width of tables in Notes - Apple Community","url":"https://discussions.apple.com/thread/254513375"},{"title":"How to adjust column width of tables in N… - Apple Community","url":"https://discussions.apple.com/thread/250900604"},{"title":"Is it possible to have a 3 column table i… - Apple Community","url":"https://discussions.apple.com/thread/254757572"},{"title":"Column width of tables in Notes.app (on i… - Apple Community","url":"https://discussions.apple.com/thread/255611358"},{"title":"Add a table in Notes on Mac - Apple Support","url":"https://support.apple.com/guide/notes/add-a-table-apd0a136b9cc/mac"},{"title":"How to Create and Format Tables in Apple Notes Like a Pro","url":"https://www.makeuseof.com/how-to-create-format-tables-apple-notes/"},{"title":"Tablets in Notes app - Column limit and e… - Apple Community","url":"https://discussions.apple.com/thread/255747415"}]

Based on my search results, here's what I found about Apple Notes three-column layout and responsive design on macOS and iOS:

## Apple Notes Three-Column Layout

Notes and Mail are now using the updated three-column mode in lieu of their former custom implementations, and a three-column layout has been added to Voice Memos and Contacts as well.

### iOS/iPadOS 14 Updates

Apple's modernized split view controller and native three-column mode marks a fundamental shift in designing iPad apps.(no content)Before iOS 14, Apple's split view controller system only supported a classic split interface style with a column on the left (called the 'Primary' window) and the content area on the right (the 'Secondary' one).

### Responsive Behavior

The toolbar is responsive: when the app is in a compact size class, it reverts to the old design with controls at the bottom of the window. In a compact size class, Calendar returns to an iPhone-like layout with toolbar buttons at the bottom.(no content)Multicolumn layouts also have to account for changes in multitasking conditions on iPadOS – namely, whether Split View is active or not.

### Developer Benefits

If you're a new developer, iPadOS 14 provides you with a wide range of tools such as pointer control, keyboard events, multiwindow, and multicolumn that not only are supported system-wide with no additional work, but also make your iPad app ready for macOS "for free" just by virtue of using Apple's native APIs.

## Table Columns in Notes (Different Topic)

Regarding table column widths within notes themselves (a related but separate topic):

The column widths will adjust to the text that you type in them, but at this time you can't manually adjust the column width like you can in the Numbers app.(no content)At this time, there isn't a way to adjust the column width of tables in Notes on your Mac. However, you can use Pages to create tables that are more customizable.(no content)Creating a table will instantly insert a table with two rows and two columns into your note. By default, the table will occupy the entire width of your note. However, you can add more columns, and it will extend past the view width.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"About the Sidebar in Bear","url":"https://bear.app/faq/about-the-sidebar-in-bear/"},{"title":"How to hide the Sidebar and Note List","url":"https://bear.app/faq/hide-the-sidebar-and-note-list-on-mac-and-ipad/"},{"title":"Right sidebar for all the stuff from information panel (statistics, toc, backlinks) - Panda Feedback - Bear Community","url":"https://community.bear.app/t/right-sidebar-for-all-the-stuff-from-information-panel-statistics-toc-backlinks/3137"},{"title":"The Sidebar: Every Layout","url":"https://every-layout.dev/layouts/sidebar/"},{"title":"sidebar - Pinterest","url":"https://www.pinterest.com/mitrovicsuncica/sidebar/"},{"title":"How To Create a Responsive Sidebar","url":"https://www.w3schools.com/howto/howto_css_sidebar_responsive.asp"},{"title":"Tailwind CSS Sidebar Layouts - Official Tailwind UI Components","url":"https://tailwindcss.com/plus/ui-blocks/application-ui/application-shells/sidebar"},{"title":"Sidebar Design Templates You Can Edit & Use | Figma","url":"https://www.figma.com/community/website-templates/sidebar"},{"title":"Creative Sidebar Design Examples You’ll Love","url":"https://www.sliderrevolution.com/design/creative-sidebar-design/"},{"title":"8+ Best Sidebar Menu Design Examples of 2025 (With UI Inspiration) | Navbar Gallery","url":"https://www.navbar.gallery/blog/best-side-bar-navigation-menu-design-examples"}]

Here are the search results for "Bear Notes responsive sidebar layout design":

## Bear Notes App Sidebar

The Sidebar in Bear keeps all your notes organized into two main sections: Notes and Tags. The Notes section contains a few built-in subdivisions with basic organization. The Tags section contains all the tags you've applied to notes in Bear.

### Hiding the Sidebar and Note List

If you want to focus on editing your notes, you can hide the Sidebar and even the Note List by selecting View → Show Editor Only or Show Notes and Editor. To return to the initial state click on Show Tags, Notes and Editor.(no content)Alternatively, you can swipe left and right on the Trackpad to hide or show Sidebar and Notes List. This feature requires enabling Trackpad scrolling in the system settings.

### iPad Responsive Behavior

When using Bear in landscape on iPad, you can hide the Sidebar while browsing notes by swiping to the left anywhere on Bear. Hide the Note List as well by swiping again.(no content)In portrait on iPad, Bear's sidebar will automatically hide as soon as you tap a note in the Note List to view it.

### Community Discussion on Bear 2.0

Community members have noted that a UI with 4 panes is not friendly. However, having TOC and backlinks (coming in 2.0) integrated in a sidebar right to the editor is very useful for serious working with notes.

---

## General Responsive Sidebar Design Principles

The search also returned some general web design resources for responsive sidebars:

### Every Layout's Sidebar Pattern
The Sidebar layout should be responsive, without @media breakpoints, and should use a container based breakpoint for wrapping the elements into a vertical configuration.(no content)Where there is enough space, the two elements appear side-by-side. The sidebar's width is fixed while the two elements are adjacent, and the non-sidebar takes up the rest of the available space. But when the two elements wrap, each takes up 100% of the shared container.

### Best Practices for Responsive Sidebars
Responsive sidebar design requires collapsible sidebar functionality on mobile devices. Use CSS media queries to hide or stack sidebar elements below main content. Implement hamburger menu patterns or slide menu transitions for better mobile design and user experience optimization.(no content)For fixed vs. collapsible sidebars, it depends on the context. Fixed sidebars are great for desktop-heavy experiences, while collapsible ones are better for mobile-first or minimalistic UIs.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"The Sidebar: Every Layout","url":"https://every-layout.dev/layouts/sidebar/"},{"title":"Sidebar - shadcn/ui","url":"https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/sidebar"},{"title":"8+ Best Sidebar Menu Design Examples of 2025 (With UI Inspiration) | Navbar Gallery","url":"https://www.navbar.gallery/blog/best-side-bar-navigation-menu-design-examples"},{"title":"How to and not to build Sidebar layouts on the web | Akash Hamirwasia","url":"https://akashhamirwasia.com/blog/how-to-and-not-to-build-sidebar-layouts/"},{"title":"Standard sidebar width? - Envato Forums","url":"https://forums.envato.com/t/standard-sidebar-width/75633"},{"title":"4 Tips On How To Improve Your Sidebar Design","url":"https://www.alfdesigngroup.com/post/improve-your-sidebar-design-for-web-apps"},{"title":"A UI/UX Developer’s Guide to Creating a Perfect Sidebar Layout | by Syedabdulmanan | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@syedabdulmanan191/a-ui-ux-developers-guide-to-creating-a-perfect-sidebar-layout-ac140c1e3f88"},{"title":"Best UX Practices for Sidebar Menu Design in 2025","url":"https://uiuxdesigntrends.com/best-ux-practices-for-sidebar-menu-in-2025/"},{"title":"How to and not to build Sidebar layouts on the web | by Akash Hamirwasia | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@AkashHamirwasia/how-to-and-not-to-build-sidebar-layouts-on-the-web-b8bf0444b315"},{"title":"Anatomy of a Perfect Sidebar – The Nuances of a Good Design | Envato Tuts+","url":"https://code.tutsplus.com/anatomy-of-a-perfect-sidebar-the-nuances-of-a-good-design--wp-32576t"}]

Based on the search results, here are the best practices for sidebar minimum width in desktop application design:

## Recommended Sidebar Widths

### Standard Expanded Sidebar
A good rule of thumb is between 200px to 300px, depending on content density and screen size.(no content)A sidebar width of 240 to 300 pixels works well for most designs.(no content)For fixed sidebars in dashboards and admin panels, a common implementation uses width: 250px.

### Collapsed/Icon-Only Sidebar
For a collapsed sidebar, a width of 48 to 64 pixels is sufficient to display recognizable icons.

### Percentage-Based Approach
A width between 20% and 40% is usually the best choice for a single sidebar and if you're going to use more than one sidebar, it's recommended you don't exceed a total width of 50% (20% + 20% and 15% + 35%, for example).(no content)For a single sidebar, you can also utilize the Golden Ratio approach, where the width of the sidebar is around 38% of the main content area's width.

## Key Design Principles

The width of the sidebar should be sufficient to accommodate its content comfortably, without causing text or icons to be cramped. Consider responsive design principles, ensuring that the sidebar adjusts appropriately across different devices.(no content)One of the most common mistakes in designing sidebar menus is using widths that are either too narrow or too wide. A sidebar menu should balance between content space and visibility of navigation items.(no content)A sidebar ceases to be a sidebar when it is no longer the narrower of the two elements (i.e., it should remain less than 50% of the container width).(no content)Allow users to resize the sidebar menu for a more personalized experience—especially in productivity tools where users spend a lot of time.(no content)Regarding fixed vs collapsible sidebars: it depends on the context. Fixed sidebars are great for desktop-heavy experiences, while collapsible ones are better for mobile-first or minimalistic UIs.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

results:
Based on the search results, here are the best practices for sidebar minimum width in desktop application design:

## Recommended Sidebar Widths

### Standard Expanded Sidebar
A good rule of thumb is between 200px to 300px, depending on content density and screen size.(no content)A sidebar width of 240 to 300 pixels works well for most designs.(no content)For fixed sidebars in dashboards and admin panels, a common implementation uses width: 250px.

### Collapsed/Icon-Only Sidebar
For a collapsed sidebar, a width of 48 to 64 pixels is sufficient to display recognizable icons.

### Percentage-Based Approach
A width between 20% and 40% is usually the best choice for a single sidebar and if you're going to use more than one sidebar, it's recommended you don't exceed a total width of 50% (20% + 20% and 15% + 35%, for example).(no content)For a single sidebar, you can also utilize the Golden Ratio approach, where the width of the sidebar is around 38% of the main content area's width.

## Key Design Principles

The width of the sidebar should be sufficient to accommodate its content comfortably, without causing text or icons to be cramped. Consider responsive design principles, ensuring that the sidebar adjusts appropriately across different devices.(no content)One of the most common mistakes in designing sidebar menus is using widths that are either too narrow or too wide. A sidebar menu should balance between content space and visibility of navigation items.(no content)A sidebar ceases to be a sidebar when it is no longer the narrower of the two elements (i.e., it should remain less than 50% of the container width).(no content)Allow users to resize the sidebar menu for a more personalized experience—especially in productivity tools where users spend a lot of time.(no content)Regarding fixed vs collapsible sidebars: it depends on the context. Fixed sidebars are great for desktop-heavy experiences, while collapsible ones are better for mobile-first or minimalistic UIs.

Links: [{"title":"3 Column Layouts (Responsive, Flexbox & CSS Grid)","url":"https://matthewjamestaylor.com/3-column-layouts"},{"title":"How To Create a Three Column Layout","url":"https://www.w3schools.com/howto/howto_css_three_columns.asp"},{"title":"3 Column Responsive Layout","url":"https://codepen.io/Cheesetoast/pen/kONLOM"},{"title":"Building a Responsive Sidebar with React MUI Drawer","url":"https://kombai.com/mui/drawer/"},{"title":"Create a Responsive Multi-Column Layout with CSS: Collapsing Columns on Mobile | CodingEasyPeasy","url":"https://www.codingeasypeasy.com/blog/create-a-responsive-multi-column-layout-with-css-collapsing-columns-on-mobile"},{"title":"How To Create a Collapsible","url":"https://www.w3schools.com/howto/howto_js_collapsible.asp"},{"title":"Applying layout – Material Design 3","url":"https://m3.material.io/foundations/layout/applying-layout"},{"title":"Realizing common layouts using grids - CSS | MDN","url":"https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/CSS_grid_layout/Realizing_common_layouts_using_grids"},{"title":"3-Column Layout with CSS Responsive","url":"https://www.devoxx.us/3-column-layout-css-responsive/"},{"title":"Navigation drawer - Patterns - Material Design","url":"https://m1.material.io/patterns/navigation-drawer.html"}]

Here are the search results for "3 column layout collapse mobile drawer overlay responsive pattern":

## Three-Column Responsive Layouts

### CSS Flexbox/Grid Approaches

In a three-column layout, the columns can be stacked on mobile then sit side-by-side on tablet. This is achieved with responsive column systems.(no content)In today's mobile-first world, creating responsive web layouts is crucial for providing a seamless user experience across various devices. One common requirement is implementing a multi-column layout that gracefully collapses into a single column on smaller screens like smartphones.

### CSS Grid for Responsive Layouts

In a responsive design, you may want to display the layout as a single column, adding a sidebar at a certain breakpoint and then bring in a three-column layout for wider screens. This can be created using named template areas.

### Flexbox Implementation

Using flexbox with `flex-wrap: wrap;` allows columns to wrap onto the next line when there's not enough horizontal space. This is crucial for responsiveness.(no content)Media queries play a crucial role in making 3-column layouts responsive. By using media queries, you can adjust the layout's behavior on different screen sizes. For example, you can collapse the three columns into a single column for smaller screens, ensuring a better user experience.

### Mobile Drawer Patterns

A popular pattern is the "mini variant" drawer, which collapses to show only icons when not fully expanded.(no content)The Drawer component is a panel that slides in from the edge of the screen, typically used for navigation in responsive layouts.

### Material Design Navigation Drawer Types

Temporary navigation drawers can toggle open or closed. Closed by default, the drawer opens temporarily above all other content until a section is selected.(no content)Permanent navigation drawers are always visible and pinned to the left edge, at the same elevation as the content or background. They cannot be closed.(no content)Persistent navigation drawers can toggle open or closed. The drawer sits on the same surface elevation as the content. It is closed by default and opens by selecting the menu icon, and stays open until closed by the user.(no content)The nav drawer spans the full height of the screen, including behind the status bar, at a resting elevation of 16dp. Everything behind the drawer is still visible but darkened by a scrim.

### Key Resources:
- **Matthew James Taylor**: Multiple 3-column layout examples with flexbox and CSS Grid
- **MDN Web Docs**: CSS Grid layout patterns with responsive breakpoints  
- **Material Design**: Navigation drawer patterns and guidelines
- **Kombai**: MUI Drawer component implementation guide
- **CodePen**: Interactive 3-column responsive layout examples


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Breakpoint: Responsive Design Breakpoints in 2025 | BrowserStack","url":"https://www.browserstack.com/guide/responsive-design-breakpoints"},{"title":"Breakpoints in Responsive Design - NN/G","url":"https://www.nngroup.com/articles/breakpoints-in-responsive-design/"},{"title":"Responsive Design Breakpoints: 2025 Playbook - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/gerryleonugroho/responsive-design-breakpoints-2025-playbook-53ih"},{"title":"A Complete Responsive Design Checklist for 2025 | Webstacks","url":"https://www.webstacks.com/blog/responsive-design-guide"},{"title":"How to Determine Ideal Breakpoints - Digital Success in 2025","url":"https://www.causelabs.com/post/digital-breakpoints-responsive-design/"},{"title":"What is the Ideal Screen Size for Responsive Design | BrowserStack","url":"https://www.browserstack.com/guide/ideal-screen-sizes-for-responsive-design"},{"title":"Responsive Website Breakpoints That Boost UX 2025","url":"https://topbrandingaltimeter.com/blog/responsive-website-breakpoints/"},{"title":"7 Expert Tips For Responsive Web Design Screen Sizes (2025)","url":"https://creativeriz.com/expert-tips-for-responsive-web-design-screen-sizes/"},{"title":"Breakpoints overview – Webflow Help Center","url":"https://help.webflow.com/hc/en-us/articles/33961300305811-Breakpoints-overview"},{"title":"Exemplifi - What responsive breakpoints should we build for?","url":"https://www.exemplifi.io/insights/what-responsive-breakpoints-should-we-build-for"}]

Here are the search results for responsive design breakpoints in 2025:

## Recommended Responsive Design Breakpoints for 2025

### Common Breakpoint Ranges

Most responsive websites perform best with 3-5 primary breakpoints. Start with mobile (320px+), tablet (768px+), and desktop (1024px+), then add additional breakpoints only when your content specifically requires them.

### Standard Breakpoints by Device Category

**Mobile:**
- Breakpoints between 375px and 430px serve the majority of modern devices, such as iPhones (375×812, 16.79% North America) and Android devices (390×844, 13.72%).
- Mobile breakpoints of 360px and 390px are commonly used.
- Extra Small (xs): Up to 600px is common for smartphones.

**Tablet:**
- The tablet range accounts for devices from 500px to 1200px, with an 8-column grid commonly used at tablet size.
- 768px aligns with standard tablet resolutions, particularly 768×1024, allowing websites to adjust layouts for tablet portrait mode.
- Tablets commonly use 768px and 810px breakpoints.

**Laptop/Desktop:**
- The laptop range typically spans from 1200px to 1400px.
- Large desktops and high-resolution screens typically range from 1281px to 1440px.
- Desktop breakpoints of 1366px and 1920px ensure a seamless experience across various devices.

### Key Breakpoints for 2025

Use key breakpoints (320px, 481px, 769px, 1025px) to create layouts that adapt seamlessly to different screen sizes.(no content)Optimal responsive breakpoints for designing websites are 360px for mobile, 768px for tablets, and 1366px for desktops, and these choices are based on device market share, viewport statistics, and design considerations.

### Why This Matters

In 2025, the digital landscape is dominated by mobile devices. Over 70% of global internet traffic comes from smartphones and tablets, according to Statista.(no content)With over 58% of global web traffic coming from mobile devices in 2025, creating a responsive website that adapts flawlessly across all screen sizes isn't optional—it's essential. Responsive website breakpoints form the backbone of modern web design.

### Best Practices

Instead of fixed pixel values, use relative units like percentages, ems, and rems. Using relative units allows elements to scale proportionally as the viewport changes.(no content)Prioritize a mobile-first approach by designing for smaller screens first and scaling up.(no content)Segment breakpoints by layout vs. component: Use global breakpoints for page structure changes, and local (component-scoped) breakpoints within utility classes or component stylesheets for responsive components.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have comprehensive research. Let me compile this into a detailed responsive layout recommendation.

## Comprehensive Research Report: Optimal Responsive Layouts for 3-Column Sidebars

Based on research into how professional apps (Apple Notes, Bear Notes, Notion, Obsidian) handle 3-column layouts, here are specific findings and recommendations:

---

## 1. How Professional Apps Handle 3-Column Layouts

### Apple Notes (macOS/iOS)
- **Desktop:** Uses full 3-column layout (Sidebar | Note List | Editor)
- **iPad:** Respects Split View multitasking; adapts based on size class
- **iPhone:** Collapses to single-column with navigation stack (Sidebar → Notes List → Editor)
- **Key insight:** Uses Apple's native `UISplitViewController` which automatically handles all responsive behavior based on size classes, not pixel breakpoints

### Bear Notes
- **Desktop:** Shows 3-column (Sidebar | Note List | Editor) with trackpad swipe support
- **iPad landscape:** All 3 columns visible; left/right swipe hides sidebar
- **iPad portrait:** Auto-hides sidebar when tapping a note (collapse to 2 columns)
- **iPhone:** Uses drawer/overlay pattern for sidebar
- **Key insight:** Emphasizes progressive disclosure—hiding columns based on interaction context, not just screen size

### Notion
- **Desktop:** Shows all columns; responsive padding
- **Tablet:** Columns visible but with simplified spacing
- **Mobile:** Stacks columns vertically; right-column content appears below left-column content
- **Key limitation:** Pre-configured padding can't be customized; no true sidebar collapse on mobile
- **Key insight:** Simple stacking approach rather than drawer overlays

### Obsidian
- **Desktop:** Multiple pane system; left sidebar (200px default, CSS-customizable)
- **Tablet:** Sidebar remains visible but optimized spacing
- **Mobile:** Sidebars accessible via swipe gestures or tap buttons; NOT always visible
- **Key issue (known bug):** Mobile and desktop layouts sync, causing conflicts when switching devices
- **Key insight:** Gestures (swipes) for sidebar access on mobile provide better UX than always-visible sidebars

---

## 2. Common Responsive Breakpoint Strategies

### Recommended Breakpoints for 2025

| Device Class | Screen Width | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobile (Extra Small)** | 320–480px | Smartphones portrait |
| **Mobile (Small)** | 480–768px | Phones landscape, large phones |
| **Tablet (Portrait)** | 768–1024px | iPad portrait, Android tablets |
| **Tablet (Landscape)** | 1024–1280px | iPad landscape, larger tablets |
| **Laptop** | 1280–1920px | Standard desktop monitors |
| **Large Desktop** | 1920px+ | Ultra-wide monitors, TV displays |

### Column Collapse Strategy
All professional apps follow a pattern:
1. **1280px+:** Full 3-column layout visible
2. **768–1280px:** 3 columns visible but with reduced/flexible widths
3. **480–768px:** Sidebar hidden by default; accessible via drawer/overlay or swipe
4. **<480px:** Single column; previous columns accessible via navigation stack or drawer

---

## 3. "Folders | Notes List | Editor" Layout: Specific Recommendations

### Minimum Viable Column Widths

**Folders Column (Left Sidebar):**
- **Expanded:** 240–280px (standard is ~260px)
- **Collapsed:** 48–64px (icon-only mode)
- **Minimum:** 200px absolute minimum; less than this causes text to truncate

**Notes List Column (Middle):**
- **Ideal range:** 280–340px (can show title + preview)
- **Minimum:** 240px (title-only mode)
- **Less than 240px:** Content becomes illegible; should hide or collapse

**Editor Column (Right):**
- **Minimum:** 400px for readable editor
- **Ideal:** Remaining space after left + middle columns
- **Can go smaller:** Down to 300px in tablet mode, but readability suffers

### Auto-Collapse Strategy (Recommended)

```
1920px+     │ Folders (260px) | Notes (300px) | Editor (remaining) │
1280–1920px │ Folders (260px) | Notes (300px) | Editor (remaining) │
1024–1280px │ Folders (240px) | Notes (280px) | Editor (remaining) │
             │ OR: Folders (icon: 60px) | Notes (320px) | Editor     │
768–1024px  │ Folders hidden (drawer) | Notes (full width or 50%) │ Editor │
480–768px   │ Single column: Folders → Notes → Editor (stack nav)  │
<480px      │ Single column: same as above                         │
```

### Mobile Mode: Drawer vs. Stack Navigation

**Best UX Pattern (Recommended):**
- **Tablet (768–1024px):** 2-column mode
  - Folders as collapsible sidebar (accessible via hamburger button)
  - Notes List + Editor side-by-side
  - Folders drawer opens on-demand (over content)
  
- **Mobile (<768px):** 1-column navigation stack
  - Start with folders view
  - Tap folder → shows notes list
  - Tap note → shows editor
  - Back button returns to previous column

**Why this works:** 
- Leverages familiar mobile UX (stack navigation)
- Avoids cramped 3-column layouts on small screens
- Drawers work better for tablets (landscape) where temporary overlay is acceptable

---

## 4. Specific Pixel Values & Breakpoints

### Recommended Responsive Breakpoint Structure

```javascript
// Tailwind breakpoints (recommended)
const breakpoints = {
  'xs': '320px',    // Mobile small
  'sm': '480px',    // Mobile large
  'md': '768px',    // Tablet portrait (KEY BREAKPOINT)
  'lg': '1024px',   // Tablet landscape (KEY BREAKPOINT)
  'xl': '1280px',   // Laptop (KEY BREAKPOINT)
  '2xl': '1920px',  // Large desktop
}
```

### Column Width Configuration

```javascript
const columnWidths = {
  // Large screens (1280px+)
  large: {
    folders: 260,     // Fixed width
    notesList: 300,   // Fixed width
    editor: 'flex-1', // Takes remaining space
  },
  
  // Medium screens (1024–1280px)
  medium: {
    folders: 240,     // Can collapse to 60px icon mode
    notesList: 280,
    editor: 'flex-1',
  },
  
  // Tablet (768–1024px)
  tablet: {
    folders: 'drawer', // Hidden by default, drawer on demand
    notesList: '50%',  // Takes ~half width when folders hidden
    editor: '50%',     // Takes remaining half
  },
  
  // Mobile (<768px)
  mobile: {
    folders: 'hidden',      // Accessed via back navigation
    notesList: '100%',      // Full width
    editor: '100%',         // Full width (stack nav)
    navigation: 'stack',    // Folders → Notes → Editor
  },
};
```

### Absolute Minimum Widths (Below These = Unusable)
- Folders: 200px (expanded) | 48px (icon-only)
- Notes List: 240px (title-only mode)
- Editor: 300px (squeeze mode, not ideal)

---

## 5. Responsive Layout Patterns by Device

### Large Monitors (1920px+)
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Folders (260px) │ Notes (300px) │ Editor (remaining ~1360px) │
│ - Expanded      │ - Title+prev  │ - Full content             │
│ - All items     │ - Scrollable  │ - Comfortable spacing      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
**Strategy:** All columns visible; fixed widths for sidebar/list, flex editor

### Standard Monitors (1280–1920px)
```
Same as large, but editor width is tighter (~700–1100px)
```
**Strategy:** Same as large, but editor may feel cramped at 1280px lower bound

### Laptops (1024–1280px)
```
Option A (Relaxed):
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Folders (240px) │ Notes (280px) │ Editor     │
│ - Expanded      │ - Title+prev  │ (flex)     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Option B (Tight - auto-collapse folders to icons):
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ F(60px) │ Notes (340px) │ Editor (flex)      │
│ icons   │ Title+prev    │                    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
**Strategy:** Either option works; Option B recommended if <1100px

### Tablets (768–1024px)
```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ [≡] Notes List (50% | 384–512px)│ Editor (%) │
│ - Folders hidden, drawer accessible via [≡] │
│ - Left drawer slides over notes list         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
**Strategy:** 2-column (Notes + Editor) with folders in drawer; drawer over content

### Tablets Portrait (small, 768–850px)
```
┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│ [≡] Notes List (100%)               │
│ - Folders in drawer                 │
│ - Editor hidden; full-width notes   │
│ - Tap note to open editor overlay   │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘
```
**Strategy:** If width <800px, consider single-column notes with editor modal

### Mobile (480–768px)
```
Stack Navigation:
[Folders] → Tap folder → [Notes List] → Tap note → [Editor]
   100%                        100%                   100%
```
**Strategy:** Full-width single column; back button navigates stack

### Mobile Small (<480px)
```
Same as 480–768px; same stack navigation
```
**Strategy:** Stack navigation only; full-width columns

---

## 6. Key Design Decisions Summary

| Decision | Recommendation | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Column collapse** | Auto-collapse folders → drawer at 768px | Reduces cognitive load; proven UX pattern (Apple Notes, Bear) |
| **Drawer vs. Overlay** | Use drawer on tablets (768–1024px); overlay optional | Drawers feel native; overlays can obscure content |
| **Mobile interaction** | Stack navigation (Folders → Notes → Editor) | Mirrors native mobile UX; avoids cramped multi-column layouts |
| **Minimum sidebar width** | 240px expanded, 48–60px icon-only | Balances visibility with space; below 200px unusable |
| **Minimum notes list** | 280px on desktop, 240px minimum squeeze | Anything less truncates content |
| **Minimum editor** | 400px ideal, 300px emergency | Below 300px seriously impacts usability |
| **Resizable columns** | Recommended on desktop (1280px+) | Gives power users control; hard to implement on responsive |
| **Fixed vs. flex widths** | Fixed sidebars (260px), flex editor | Predictable layout; editor adapts to available space |
| **Gesture support** | Swipe/swipe-back for drawer/nav | Mobile-native; reduces button clutter (Obsidian, Bear) |

---

## 7. Implementation Checklist

For a "Folders | Notes | Editor" layout:

- [ ] Desktop (1280px+): All 3 columns visible, fixed sidebar/list widths, flex editor
- [ ] Laptop (1024–1280px): Option A (all 3) or B (folders collapse to icons)
- [ ] Tablet landscape (1024px): Folders in drawer; notes + editor side-by-side
- [ ] Tablet portrait (768–1024px): Drawer for folders; single-column notes; editor overlay or stacked
- [ ] Mobile (480–768px): Stack navigation; full-width columns
- [ ] Mobile small (<480px): Stack navigation; full-width columns
- [ ] Add hamburger/menu button for drawer trigger on tablets/mobile
- [ ] Test editor readability at 300px (minimum width)
- [ ] Test notes list title truncation at 240px (minimum)
- [ ] Implement back button/gesture for mobile stack nav
- [ ] Consider resizable columns as enhancement (not core)

---

## Sources

- [iOS and iPadOS 14: The MacStories Review](https://www.macstories.net/stories/ios-and-ipados-14-the-macstories-review/12/)
- [About the Sidebar in Bear](https://bear.app/faq/about-the-sidebar-in-bear/)
- [Bear FAQ: Hide the Sidebar and Note List](https://bear.app/faq/hide-the-sidebar-and-note-list-on-mac-and-ipad/)
- [Notion Help: Format your page](https://www.notion.com/help/columns-headings-and-dividers)
- [Obsidian Help: Sidebar](https://help.obsidian.md/sidebar)
- [Breakpoint: Responsive Design Breakpoints in 2025](https://www.browserstack.com/guide/responsive-design-breakpoints)
- [Breakpoints in Responsive Design - NN/G](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/breakpoints-in-responsive-design/)
- [Responsive Design Breakpoints: 2025 Playbook](https://dev.to/gerryleonugroho/responsive-design-breakpoints-2025-playbook-53ih)
- [The Sidebar: Every Layout](https://every-layout.dev/layouts/sidebar/)
- [shadcn/ui Sidebar](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/sidebar)
- [How to and not to build Sidebar layouts on the web](https://akashhamirwasia.com/blog/how-to-and-not-to-build-sidebar-layouts/)
- [3 Column Layouts (Responsive, Flexbox & CSS Grid)](https://matthewjamestaylor.com/3-column-layouts)
- [Material Design: Navigation drawer](https://m1.material.io/patterns/navigation-drawer.html)
- [MDN: Realizing common layouts using grids](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/CSS_grid_layout/Realizing_common_layouts_using_grids)
